title: Asymptotic dimension, Isoperimetric Problem, and Traveling Salesman Problem in Groups
speaker: Anna Erschler
abstract:
In many groups, the optimal diameter for the solutions of the isoperimetric problem is asymptotically equivalent to the lower bound coming from their cardinality; in this situation one says that the Følner function is lossless with respect to the isodiametric function. Answering a question of Nowak, we prove that for some   groups the Følner function is not lossless, and we compute the asymptotics of the isodiametric function in many examples.
We study the relationship between the isodiametric function, the control function for the asymptotic dimension of the group, and an invariant related to the Universal Traveling Salesman Problem. We prove that the isodiametric function is equivalent to the control function for the asymptotic dimension for a broad class of groups with a lossless Følner function. In particular, this applies to the diagonal products of Brieussel–Zheng associated with a sequence of expander graphs. 
For our main examples, the Følner function is not lossless with respect to the isodiametric function; nevertheless, we show that in these examples the control function is lossless with respect to the isodiametric one. Such groups include wreath products, Hall’s nilpotent-by-abelian groups, and Lampshuffler groups. While many amenable groups of finite Assouad–Nagata dimension have a lossless control function, we also construct examples with a non-lossless control function.
We also discuss open problems concerning how the isoperimetric problem influences algebraic properties of groups. We show that every amenable group of finite Assouad–Nagata dimension has Shalom’s property HFD. In particular, such groups cannot be simple and cannot be purely torsion. The first conclusion contrasts with the simple groups of Burger and Mozes, some of which are quasi-isometric to a product of two trees and therefore have Assouad–Nagata dimension two.

proof_stages.label: Cayley-graph Følner and isodiametric setup
proof_stages.summary: The talk fixes a finitely generated group through its Cayley graph and defines the usual Følner function and the isodiametric Følner function by minimizing, respectively, the size and the diameter of sets with boundary-to-volume ratio at most 1/n.

proof_stages.label: Growth calibration and limits of Følner data
proof_stages.summary: Boundary conventions, polynomial-growth balls, subexponential and exponential growth questions, and Coulhon-type lower bounds are reviewed to show that Følner functions are governed mainly by growth and do not by themselves recover dimension.

proof_stages.label: Asymptotic dimension and control functions
proof_stages.summary: Asymptotic dimension is introduced via covers of bounded multiplicity that absorb R-balls, together with the associated control function and the special linear-control case of finite Assouad--Nagata dimension.

proof_stages.label: Comparison problem and losslessness
proof_stages.summary: The central comparison is formulated: in many groups the isodiametric Følner function appears equivalent to the asymptotic-dimension control function, while the inverse growth lower bound motivates the notion of a lossless isodiametric Følner function.

proof_stages.label: Computed families and lossless/nonlossless phenomena
proof_stages.summary: A table of group families is presented, including lamplighter-type wreath products, finite Assouad--Nagata dimension groups, prescribed-Følner-function constructions, solvable groups, and shuffler/traveler groups, showing many coincidences of control and isodiametric scales but also nonlossless examples and emerging separations.

proof_stages.label: Universal traveling-salesman comparison
proof_stages.summary: The universal TSP problem is recalled as the problem of choosing one global order whose induced tour on every finite subset has small competitive ratio, with constant behavior for trees/hyperbolic spaces, logarithmic behavior for the plane and doubling or finite Assouad--Nagata spaces, and conjectural converse statements.

proof_stages.label: Elongation-of-snakes invariant
proof_stages.summary: The speaker defines a new order-dependent snake-elongation function by comparing the length of separated ordered configurations to their width, minimizes it over orders, and states that in many of the studied groups it matches the control function, with no known counterexample to this equivalence.

proof_stages.label: Structural consequences and open algebraic questions
proof_stages.summary: The final stage asks what algebraic information can be recovered from these large-scale functions, recalling Nowak-type implications, stating that amenable groups of finite asymptotic dimension have Shalom's property HFD and hence are not pure torsion or simple, and posing broader elementary-amenability and prescribed-growth questions.

examples.name: Abelian Cayley lattices and boundary conventions
examples.note: Abelian groups, especially the lattice pictures for Z and Z^2, make the competing boundary notions and the n^d Følner scale concrete without driving the later comparison theorems.
examples.engagement_reason: Abelian groups and Z^2 lattice boundary conventions are cited with stated facts about optimal shapes and asymptotic equivalence, but no boundary computation or verification is carried out.

passes.reason: ordinary abelian Cayley-lattice examples illustrate boundary conventions and asymptotic Følner behavior
Abelian Cayley lattices are used as a standard amenable test family for boundary conventions, not as a sporadic or adversarial case.

examples.name: Nilpotent group of dimension three and growth degree four
examples.note: The standard example alluded to is a nilpotent group such as the discrete Heisenberg group, used to show that polynomial growth degree, which controls Følner functions, is not the same as geometric dimension.
examples.engagement_reason: The dimension-three, growth-degree-four nilpotent case is invoked only to illustrate that growth degree need not equal dimension.

passes.reason: a standard Heisenberg-type nilpotent group shows that polynomial growth degree need not equal geometric dimension

examples.name: Line metric for universal TSP
examples.note: On a line, visiting points from left to right gives the exact traveling-salesman path, serving as the simplest benchmark for the universal-ordering problem.
examples.engagement_reason: For the line metric, the speaker applies the universal TSP ordering idea and verifies that visiting points left-to-right gives a shortest path.

examples.name: Tree metric traversal for universal TSP
examples.note: A depth-first traversal order around a tree gives only constant multiplicative error, illustrating why trees and hyperbolic spaces are best-case settings for universal TSP.
examples.engagement_reason: For a tree metric, the speaker describes the traversal order around the tree and reasons that its path length is within a constant factor of optimal.

examples.name: Z^2 universal TSP: lexicographic failure and space-filling order
examples.note: The bad zigzags produced by lexicographic order on two vertical strings of lattice points visibly force the space-filling-curve ordering idea that yields the classical logarithmic upper bound.
examples.engagement_reason: For Z^2, the speaker analyzes why lexicographic order causes k^2 zigzags versus linear optimum and then constructs a space-filling-style order with logarithmic error.

passes.reason: the regular Z^2 grid is used with an adversarial two-column configuration to show lexicographic ordering fails, contrasted with a space-filling order
On the regular square lattice Z^2, lexicographic ordering creates zig-zag tours of order k^2 versus optimal order k, refuting that naive order while motivating the space-filling order.
The square lattice is regular, and the lexicographic two-column configuration is used as an adversarial failure case.
the Z^2 lexicographic-order construction is used as an adversarial failure producing zigzags much longer than the optimal tour
